title: Membrane transport
description: Test your knowledge of membrane transport!

assessment_items.question_content:
The model below depicts the movement of several ions and molecules across a cell membrane. For each ion or molecule, the relative concentration on each side of the membrane is shown.

[[Ōśā image 1]]

**Which of the following statements accurately describes one of the movements shown in the model?**

[[Ōśā radio 1]]

[[Ōśā explanation 1]]

options.alt: A phospholipid bilayer with 3 membrane proteins with channels is shown. One side of the phospholipid bilayer is labeled solution surrounding cell and the other side of the phospholipid bilayer is labeled cytoplasm. The first membrane protein is labeled with high sodium ions in the solution surrounding the cell and an arrow pointing through the channel to the label low sodium ions in the solution surrounding the cell. The second protein channel has two arrows in it. One arrow is labeled low sodium ions in the cytoplasm and points to high sodium ions in the solution surrounding the cell. The other arrow shows low potassium ions in the solution surrounding the cell and points to high potassium ions in the cytoplasm. The third membrane protein is labeled high chloride ions in the cytoplasm and an arrow pointing through the channel to the label low chloride ions in the solution surrounding the cell. Next to the third membrane protein there is an arrow that points from high carbon dioxide in the cytoplasm. The arrow goes through the phospholipid bilayer and points to low carbon dioxide in the solution surrounding the cell.

choices.content: $\text{Na}^+$ is transported out of the cell by active transport.
$\text{CO}_2$ movement out of the cell requires ATP hydrolysis.
$\text{Cl}^-$ is transported out of the cell by active transport.
$\text{Na}^+$ movement into the cell requires ATP hydrolysis.

assessment_items.question_content:
The model below depicts the movement of several ions and molecules across a cell membrane. For each ion or molecule, the relative concentration on each side of the membrane is shown.

[[Ōśā image 1]]

**Which of the following statements accurately describes one of the movements shown in the model?**

[[Ōśā radio 1]]

[[Ōśā explanation 1]]

options.alt: A phospholipid bilayer with 3 membrane proteins with channels is shown. One side of the phospholipid bilayer is labeled solution surrounding cell and the other side of the phospholipid bilayer is labeled cytoplasm. The first membrane protein is labeled with high glucose in the solution surrounding the cell and an arrow pointing through the channel to the label low glucose in the solution surrounding the cell. The second protein channel has an arrow pointing from low calcium ions in the cytoplasm through the protein channel towards high calcium ions in the solution surrounding the cell.  The third membrane protein is labeled high potassium ions in the cytoplasm and an arrow pointing through the channel to the label low potassium ions in the solution surrounding the cell. Next to the third membrane protein there is an arrow that points from high oxygen in the solution surrounding the cell. The arrow goes through the phospholipid bilayer and points to low oxygen in the cytoplasm.

choices.content: $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ is transported out of the cell by active transport.
$\text{O}_2$ is transported into the cell by active transport.
Glucose movement into the cell requires ATP hydrolysis.
$\text{K}^+$ movement out of the cell requires ATP hydrolysis.

assessment_items.question_content:
The model below depicts the movement of several ions and molecules across a cell membrane. For each ion or molecule, the relative concentration on each side of the membrane is shown.

[[Ōśā image 1]]

**Which of the following statements accurately describes one of the movements shown in the model?**

[[Ōśā radio 1]]

[[Ōśā explanation 1]]

options.alt: A phospholipid bilayer with 4 membrane proteins with channels is shown. One side of the phospholipid bilayer is labeled solution surrounding cell and the other side of the phospholipid bilayer is labeled cytoplasm. The first membrane protein is labeled with high potassium in the cytoplasm and an arrow pointing through the channel to the label low potassium in the solution surrounding the cell. The second membrane protein is labeled with low chloride ions in the cytoplasm and an arrow pointing through the channel to the label high chloride ions in the solution surrounding the cell. The third protein channel has two arrows in it. One arrow is labeled low hydrogen ions in the cytoplasm and points to high hydrogen ions in the solution surrounding the cell. The other arrow shows low potassium ions in the solution surrounding the cell and points to high potassium ions in the cytoplasm. The fourth protein channel has an arrow pointing from high fructose in the cytoplasm towards low fructose in the solution surrounding the cell.

choices.content: $\text{K}^+$ movement into the cell requires ATP hydrolysis.
$\text{K}^+$ is transported out of the cell by active transport.
$\text{Cl}^-$ is transported out of the cell by active transport.
Fructose movement out of the cell requires ATP hydrolysis.

assessment_items.question_content:
The model below depicts the movement of several ions and molecules across a cell membrane. For each ion or molecule, the relative concentration on each side of the membrane is shown.

[[Ōśā image 1]]

**Which of the following statements accurately describes one of the movements shown in the model?**

[[Ōśā radio 1]]

[[Ōśā explanation 1]]

options.alt: A phospholipid bilayer with 4 membrane proteins with channels is shown. One side of the phospholipid bilayer is labeled solution surrounding cell and the other side of the phospholipid bilayer is labeled cytoplasm. The first membrane protein is labeled with low hydrogen ions in the cytoplasm and an arrow pointing through the channel to the label high hydrogen ions in the solution surrounding the cell. The second membrane protein is labeled with low calcium ions in the cytoplasm and an arrow pointing through the channel to the label high calcium ions in the solution surrounding the cell. The third protein channel has two arrows in it. One arrow is labeled low sodium ions in the cytoplasm and points to high sodium ions in the solution surrounding the cell. The other arrow shows low potassium ions in the solution surrounding the cell and points to high potassium ions in the cytoplasm. The fourth protein channel has an arrow pointing from high glucose in the solution surrounding the cell through the channel towards low glucose in the cytoplasm.

choices.content: Glucose moves into the cell by passive transport.
$\text{Na}^+$ moves out of the cell by passive transport.
$\text{H}^+$ movement out of the cell does not require an input of energy.
$\text{Ca}^{2+}$ movement out of the cell does not require an input of energy.

assessment_items.question_content:
The model below shows how glucose, sodium ions ($\text{Na}^+$), and potassium ions ($\text{K}^+$) move between the intestinal lumen, an epithelial cell, and the blood in the small intestine. The relative concentrations of glucose, $\text{Na}^+$, and $\text{K}^+$ inside and outside the epithelial cell are indicated.

[[Ōśā image 1]]

**Based on the model, which of the following movements does *not* require an input of energy?**

[[Ōśā radio 1]]

[[Ōśā explanation 1]]

options.alt: A diagram of an epithelial cell with the caption high potassium ions, low sodium ions, and high glucose. On the bottom of the cell is the label blood with the caption low potassium ions, high sodium ions and low glucose. On the top of the cell is the label intestinal tubule lumen with the caption high sodium ions, medium glucose. Between the epithelial cell and the intestinal lumen there is a blue circle with 2 arrows pointing from the intestinal lumen into the epithelial cell. One arrow is labeled sodium and the other arrow is labeled glucose. Between the epithelial cell and the blood there are 2 circles. The first circle is red and has 2 arrows. One arrow is pointing from the epithelial cell towards the blood and is labeled sodium ions. The other arrow is pointing from the blood towards the epithelial cell and is labeled potassium ions. The second circle is blue and has an arrow pointing from the epithelial cell towards the blood and is labeled glucose ions.

choices.content: glucose moving out of an epithelial cell into the blood
glucose moving out of the intestinal lumen into an epithelial cell
$\text{Na}^+$ moving out of an epithelial cell into the blood
$\text{K}^+$ moving out of the blood into an epithelial cell

assessment_items.question_content:
The model below shows how potassium ions ($\text{K}^+$), sodium ions ($\text{Na}^+$), and hydrogen ions ($\text{H}^+$) move between the blood, a parietal cell, and the gastric pit lumen in the stomach. The relative concentrations of $\text{K}^+$, $\text{Na}^+$, and $\text{H}^+$ inside and outside the parietal cell are indicated.

[[Ōśā image 1]]

**Based on the model, which of the following movements does *not* require an input of energy?**

[[Ōśā radio 1]]

[[Ōśā explanation 1]]

options.alt: A diagram of a parietal cell with the caption high potassium ions, low sodium ions and low hydrogen ions. The left of the cell is labeled blood with the caption low potassium ions and high sodium ions. On the cell membrane of the parietal cell next to the blood is a red circle with an arrow pointing from the blood into the parietal cell labeled potassium ions. There is a second arrow in the circle pointing from the parietal cell towards the blood labeled sodium ions. On the right side of the parietal cell the area is labeled gastric pit lumen and has the caption low potassium ions and high sodium ions. The cell membrane of the parietal cell facing the gastric pit lumen is irregular in shape and creates folds that form 2 pits. There are 2 circles on the parietal cell next to the gastric pit lumen. One circle is purple and has two arrows. One of the arrows points from the gastric pit lumen towards the parietal cell and is labeled potassium ions. The second arrow points from the parietal cell towards the gastric pit lumen and is labeled hydrogen ions. The second circle on the parietal cell is pink and has an arrow pointing from the parietal cell towards the gastric pit lumen and is labeled potassium ions.

choices.content: $\text{K}^+$ moving out of a parietal cell into the gastric pit lumen
$\text{H}^+$ moving out of a parietal cell into the gastric pit lumen
$\text{Na}^+$ moving out of a parietal cell into the blood
$\text{K}^+$ moving out of the blood into a parietal cell

assessment_items.question_content:
The model below shows how several ions and molecules move between the proximal tubule lumen, an epithelial cell, and the blood in the kidney. The relative concentrations of sodium ions ($\text{Na}^+$), potassium ions ($\text{K}^+$), and calcium ions ($\text{Ca}^{2+}$) inside and outside the epithelial cell are indicated.

[[Ōśā image 1]]

**Based on the model, which of the following movements *requires* an input of energy?**

[[Ōśā radio 1]]

[[Ōśā explanation 1]]

options.alt: A diagram of an epithelial cell with the caption high potassium ions, low sodium ions, and high calcium ions. On the left of the cell is the label blood with the caption low potassium ions, high sodium ions and low calcium ions. On the right of the cell is the label proximal tubule lumen with the caption low potassium ions and high sodium ions. Between the epithelial cell and the proximal tubule lumen there is a blue circle with 2 arrows pointing from the proximal tubule lumen into the epithelial cell. One arrow is labeled sodium and the other arrow is labeled glucose. Between the epithelial cell and the blood there are 3 circles. The first circle is red and has 2 arrows.  One arrow is pointing from the epithelial cell towards the blood and is labeled sodium ions. The other arrow is pointing from the blood towards the epithelial cell and is labeled potassium ions. The second circle is brown and has an arrow pointing from the epithelial cell towards the blood and is labeled calcium ions. The third circle is orange and has an arrow pointing from the epithelial cell towards the blood and is labeled potassium ions.

choices.content: $\text{Na}^+$ moving out of an epithelial cell into the blood
$\text{K}^+$ moving out of an epithelial cell into the blood
$\text{Ca}^{2+}$ moving out of an epithelial cell into the blood
$\text{Na}^+$ moving out of the proximal tubule lumen into an epithelial cell

assessment_items.question_content:
Viruses are infectious particles that usually consist of DNA or RNA inside a protein coat. A virus often hijacks a host cellŌĆÖs own processes in order to cross the plasma membrane and gain entry into the cell. Once inside, the virus uses the cellŌĆÖs parts and metabolism to multiply. 

Scientists studying a virus known as Semliki Forest virus (SFV) performed an experiment to learn about the mechanism by which SFV crosses the host cell plasma membrane. For the experiment, the scientists used two groups of cultured human cells. First, they applied chlorpromazine, a chemical that interferes with a certain cell process, to one of the groups. Then, they exposed both groups to equal amounts of SFV. The scientists observed that the virus was only able to enter the cells that had *not* been exposed to chlorpromazine.

**Based on the experiment described above, which of the following cell processes does chlorpromazine most likely inhibit?**

[[Ōśā radio 1]]

[[Ōśā explanation 1]]
hints: The results of the experiment suggest that chlorpromazine interferes with the cell process that SFV uses to enter a cell.

The text tells us that viruses like SFV are made up of DNA or RNA and proteins. These types of large macromolecules typically move across the plasma membrane by receptor-mediated endocytosis, a process in which molecules are enclosed in membrane-bound vesicles and transported into the cell.
So, chlorpromazine likely inhibits the cell process of receptor-mediated endocytosis.

options.explanation: Sieczkarski, Sara B, and Gary R Whittaker. 2002. ŌĆ£Influenza virus can enter and infect cells...ŌĆØ *Journal of virology* 76(20):10455-64. https://doi.org/10.1128/jvi.76.20.10455-10464.2002.

choices.content: Receptor-mediated endocytosis
Membrane transport mediated by transmembrane ATPases
Vesicle fusion during exocytosis
Facilitated diffusion through membrane water channels

assessment_items.question_content:
A neuron stimulates muscle contraction by sending signals across the neuromuscular junction, or the point of contact between a neuron and a muscle cell. The signaling process begins when membrane-bound structures inside the neuron fuse with the cell membrane, releasing signaling molecules into the neuromuscular junction. These molecules then diffuse through the junction and binds to receptors on the surface of the muscle cell, leading to muscle contraction. 

Botulism is a rare illness caused by a toxin produced by the bacterium *Clostridium botulinum*. This toxin, called botulinum toxin, inhibits the process by which signaling molecules are released from neurons at the neuromuscular junction. This disrupts the neuron-muscle cell signaling pathway, resulting in temporary paralysis.

**Based on the information above, which of the following cell processes does botulinum toxin most likely inhibit?**

[[Ōśā radio 1]]
hints: According to the text, botulinum toxin inhibits the cell process by which signaling molecules are released from neurons.

The text tells us that signaling molecules are released from neurons when membrane-bound structures inside the neuron fuse with the cell membrane. This is consistent with what occurs during exocytosis, a type of bulk transport. In exocytosis, vesicles fuse with the cell membrane and release their contents into the extracellular environment.
So, botulinum toxin most likely inhibits vesicle fusion during exocytosis.

choices.content: Vesicle fusion during exocytosis
Receptor-mediated endocytosis
Membrane transport mediated by transmembrane ATPases
Facilitated diffusion through membrane channels

assessment_items.question_content:
Root hairs are tubular outgrowths of root epidermal cells in plants. These outgrowths help plants absorb water and nutrients by increasing root surface area.

Root hairs form when membrane-bound structures inside root epidermal cells carry macromolecules to one side of the cell. There, the structures fuse with the plasma membrane, releasing their contents outside the cell. The released macromolecules are then incorporated into the elongating cell wall of the root epidermal cell, which allows the root hair to continue growing. 

A group of scientists discovered a plant that had no root hairs. The scientists determined that this phenotype was due to a genetic mutation that inhibited the process by which root hair growth takes place. This mutation was located in the *rth1* gene.

**Based on the information above, which of the following cell processes did the *rth1* mutation most likely inhibit in the plant with no root hairs?**

[[Ōśā radio 1]]

[[Ōśā explanation 1]]
hints: The *rth1* mutation inhibited the cell process by which root hair growth takes place. According to the text, root hair growth occurs as macromolecules are released from one side of root epidermal cells.

The text tells us that macromolecules are released from root epidermal cells when membrane-bound structures inside the cells fuse with the plasma membrane. This describes vesicle fusion during exocytosis, which is a type of bulk transport.
So, the mutation in *rth1* likely inhibited the cell process of vesicle fusion during exocytosis.

options.explanation: Wen, Tsui-Jung, Frank Hochholdinger, Michaela Sauer, Wesley Bruce, and Patrick S. Schnable. 2005. The roothairless1 Gene of Maize Encodes a Homolog of sec3, Which Is Involved inŌĆ”ŌĆØ *Plant Physiology* 138(3):1637-43. https://doi.org/10.1104/pp.105.062174.

choices.content: Vesicle fusion during exocytosis
Receptor-mediated endocytosis
Membrane transport mediated by transmembrane ATPases
Facilitated diffusion through membrane channels
